items.objective: Read a few short Kyrgyz words in Cyrillic and connect them to sound and meaning.
items.instructions: Look at the Kyrgyz word first. Use transliteration only when you need a quick pronunciation check.
successCriteria: Recognize an easy A-word in Cyrillic.
Recognize at least one word with `ө` or `ү`.
Connect a survival word like `Ооба` or `Жок` to meaning without translating letter by letter forever.
items.completionHint: Spot the overall word shape first, then zoom into the special letters.

supportCards.title: Letters can predict later suffix patterns
supportCards.body: When you notice `ө` or `ү`, you are not just memorizing a symbol. You are also training your eye for a front-vowel pattern that matters in later grammar.

supportCards.title: Use transliteration as a quick check, not your first stop
supportCards.body: Read the Cyrillic once before peeking. A two-second attempt builds script confidence much faster than immediate transliteration dependence.

supportCards.title: Apricot is a strong Central Asian starter word
supportCards.body: `Өрүк` means apricot. Apricots show up often in Central Asian food culture, so this small reading card quietly doubles as a regional culture anchor.

items.objective: Notice which word contains the special Kyrgyz ng letter and which words show the front-vowel family.
items.instructions: Read each item and choose the one that matches the sound clue.
successCriteria: Find a word with a front rounded vowel.
Say why the special letter matters for pronunciation.
items.completionHint: Check the middle of the word carefully. `ң` is easy to miss if you only skim the first letter.

supportCards.title: Treat `ң` as its own building block
supportCards.body: If you split `ң` into `n` plus `g`, the word will sound wrong. Kyrgyz learners do better when they memorize it as one fixed sound block from the start.

supportCards.title: Round your lips for `ө` and `ү`
supportCards.body: A quick pronunciation trick: keep your tongue forward and round your lips a bit for `ө` and `ү`. That helps them feel distinct from plain `о` and `у`.

supportCards.title: One extra letter can change a whole identity cue
supportCards.body: Words with `ң` often become instant identity markers for learners because that sound is less common in many European languages. Nailing it early makes your Kyrgyz sound noticeably more grounded.

items.prompt: Which word contains the single ng sound letter, and which word shows a front rounded vowel?

targets.focus: Good survival phrase, but no special front-vowel or ng target here.

items.objective: Read a short encounter and pick the right survival responses in Cyrillic.
items.instructions: Complete the exchange with the best short reply for each moment.
successCriteria: Choose `Ооба` or `Жок` to match the question.
items.completionHint: Start with the social phrase, then choose the yes-no word that fits the prompt.

supportCards.title: Social-survival words deserve early automaticity
supportCards.body: Very short words like `Ооба`, `Жок`, and `Рахмат` are worth overlearning. They let the learner participate before full sentence grammar is available.

supportCards.title: Give `Рахмат` a clear back `kh` sound
supportCards.body: Do not flatten `Рахмат` into English `rah-mat`. Let the middle consonant stay a little rougher in the throat.

supportCards.title: Short formulas carry a lot of social weight
supportCards.body: In many Central Asian settings, even very short respectful formulas matter. Getting a greeting and thanks right can smooth interaction long before your grammar catches up.
